Import a story

Reads your prose and proposes the beings, places, objects, and events to review and approve before the story is built. For a longer work, ingest one chapter or part at a time — each becomes a section on the timeline. Elements stay in this story unless you mark them for all stories.

Support Dystoria

Dystoria is free while it’s in early access. If it’s been useful to you, you can pay what you feel it’s worth — every bit goes straight into building it.

Send Feedback

Hit a bug, or have an idea? Tell me anything — it comes straight to me.

About

Dystoria

Out of the chaos comes coherence.

Do you struggle to wrestle your story ideas into a coherent, structured narrative? Do you have tons of notes and half-started ideas you need inspiration to pick up again and re-forge? Dystoria helps you organise your labyrinth of narrative elements and turn it into a map — and then take practical steps to explore the most exciting path through it, charting the journey of discovery of your story within that web of ideas.

All living things exist and grow in a cycle of mini-deaths and rebirths. Dystoria is designed as a loop — a cycle of iterations that make the process of writing alive, fun and generative.

AI is incorporated not as a crutch, but as a binding agent for your ideas and the process. It creates a wiki from your goals, notes, elements and prose to keep all those ideas organized. It asks you questions and reflects your progress back to you against your intent. You are the creator, and it is your assistant.

How it works — a loop, not a line

Writing is rarely tidy. Ideas arrive faster than they can be ordered; a draft reveals what the outline could not; an edit raises a question the opening never answered. Dystoria is built for that reality — a cycle, a series of iterations that make the process of writing alive, fun and generative.

The five modes — Map, Write, Refine, Read and Wiki — each take charge of one phase, from brainstorming to drafting to refinement. But none is a dead end. What you discover while writing flows back to the map; what the wiki notices flows back into your next session. The work spirals inward, tightening toward a finished, coherent story.

Phase 1 · Brainstorm & organizeMap

Map is where intent becomes structure. Don’t just list characters — plot their motivations. Every element — a being, a setting, an object, a concept — lives on an interactive map with its own notepad. Generate elements when you want a spark, or add your own; drag them into relationships; set the Narrative Overlay (genre, concepts, context) that frames the whole world. This is the home of the labyrinth of ideas, where the chaos is allowed to exist — and where you begin turning it into a path you can walk.

Phase 2 · Focused writingWrite

Write is a minimal, full-screen space where the tool hands you the spark. Choose one or two focus elements, optionally add a guide or exercise, and set a timer — a sprint, a deeper block, or an open marathon. Dystoria generates a one-line prompt rooted in your story’s DNA: the focus elements, your notes about them, and the prose so far. Previous sessions are tucked away so only the blank, beckoning line remains. When the clock ends, the words lock into place.

Phase 3 · Edit & refineRefine

Refine is where you edit without fear. Highlight any passage to rephrase it, generate alternative sentences, or leave a suggested change to accept or revisit later. Experiment with prose styles and leave notes for the writer you’ll be tomorrow. Refine is a conversation with your future self — the draft made malleable, not fragile.

Phase 4 · Perspective & feedbackRead

Read stitches the whole story together, distraction-free, so you can experience it as a reader would. Compare versions with the Suggestions / Original toggle, and — when you’re ready — share a link to gather feedback. Each reader sees only their own notes; you see every insight back in your workspace.

Phase 5 · Synthesis & questionsWiki

Wiki closes the loop. One step synthesizes your prose, elements, notes and goals into a living Story Wiki — and it doesn’t just summarize. It surfaces the most pressing open questions your story raises. Those questions travel: each is raised quietly in the notepad of the element it concerns, and — perhaps one time in three — it becomes the spark for your next session. When your prose answers a question, the next pass marks it resolved.

How the loops close

Map → Write. The elements and intentions you shape become the focus and the spark of your sessions.

Write → Refine → Read. What you draft becomes what you sharpen, then what you finally see whole.

Read / Refine → Wiki. Finishing a session, or crossing a threshold of edits, refreshes the wiki — quietly in the background, or whenever you ask.

Wiki → Map → Write. The questions the wiki raises return to your elements and reappear as the prompt that starts your next session. Round after round, the loops tighten — toward coherence.

What the loops tighten around

At the center of the spiral, ideally, is your story goal — what kind of story this is, how it should feel, who it’s for. When a goal exists it becomes the gravity of the whole system: the prompts bend toward it, and the wiki’s first questions test the story against it.

And if there is no goal — if you’d rather discover the story by writing it — nothing breaks. The tool holds instead to the elements and the prose themselves, using what is actually on the page and the map to drive a coherent structure.

You hold the pen

AI in Dystoria is not a crutch, but a binding agent for your ideas and your process. It keeps everything organized, asks the questions lingering behind your decisions, and offers a spark when the page is blank. But you are the creator, and it is your assistant — every element, every sentence, every decision about what your story means is yours.

Out of the chaos comes coherence.

Keep building?

Feel free to keep using Dystoria without an account — this one Story file is yours to experiment with and export. But to build a Library of stories, grow a Wiki, and unlock AI-supported features, you’ll want an account. It’s still free — and any donation helps keep Dystoria alive and improving.
